decisions.title: Astro over Next.js
decisions.context: The site is mostly static, SEO-critical content read by recruiters and peers on commutes, not an app that needs client-side routing state.
decisions.decision: Astro 5 with React islands only where interactivity is real (chat, forms, theme toggle), instead of Next.js.
decisions.cost: Gave up Next's larger ecosystem and app-router conveniences. Bought near-zero client JS on every content page by default — a budget Next would need deliberate work to hit, since its defaults ship a client-first runtime whether a page needs one or not.

decisions.title: Git-based CMS over a hosted one
decisions.context: No evidence yet that a plain git-plus-editor workflow can't keep up with actual publishing cadence, before paying for a hosted CMS to solve a problem that might not exist.
decisions.decision: Content lives as git-versioned MDX/JSON, read through one ContentRepository port, edited three equivalent ways — direct file, Claude Code, or a local Keystatic UI.
decisions.cost: Gave up a hosted UI's polish and multi-editor collaboration. Bought a $0 CMS with the swap to Sanity or Payload staying a single new adapter file, not a page-level rewrite, if the workflow ever does hit a ceiling.

decisions.title: Workers AI first, Anthropic as fallback
decisions.context: The chatbot needs a model provider, and a paid API as the default breaks the $0/month budget the moment the site gets any real traffic.
decisions.decision: A provider router tries Cloudflare Workers AI first, free and colocated with the rest of the stack, and falls back to Anthropic only if that call errors — never the other way around.
decisions.cost: Workers AI quality trails frontier models on nuanced questions. The tradeoff: a chatbot that costs nothing at normal traffic, with the upgrade to Claude Haiku sitting behind one config change in src/config/llm.ts, not a rewrite.

decisions.title: Cloudflare's free tier as the ceiling, not a starting point
decisions.context: Domain already owned; every other cost (hosting, embeddings, rate-limit storage, email) had to fit inside a tier that won't get bumped by a personal site's real traffic, not just look cheap on a demo.
decisions.decision: Pages/Workers for hosting, KV for rate limiting, Vectorize for embeddings, Workers AI for the chatbot — one provider, one free tier, for everything that isn't the domain itself.
decisions.cost: Gave up a multi-vendor stack that might optimize any one piece slightly better. Bought a $0/month recurring bill regardless of how many features get added, because they all share the same headroom instead of each adding its own bill.

decisions.title: The hero moved from decorative to data-true
decisions.context: An early hero concept was a generic animated gradient mesh with an optional pointer-parallax dot grid: motion for its own sake, no connection to what the site is actually about.
decisions.decision: Replaced it with a living-blueprint diagram generated at build time from the real competency matrix — nodes are skill domains weighted by real depth, edges are roles that touched them. Not decorative randomness.
decisions.cost: More build complexity than a static gradient. The hero now changes only when the underlying career data changes — the diagram is a claim about real experience, not a mood board.

decisions.title: A hand-written fetch transport over the MCP SDK's own
decisions.context: The MCP server's Cloudflare Worker transport needed to implement the protocol's Transport interface, and the SDK ships one, StreamableHTTPServerTransport, for exactly this.
decisions.decision: Traced that class into node_modules and found it's typed and implemented directly against Node's IncomingMessage/ServerResponse, which don't exist in the Workers runtime. Wrote FetchTransport instead: the same interface, implemented directly over the standard Fetch API.
decisions.cost: Gave up the SDK's official, more-tested transport implementation. Bought a Worker that actually runs, verified with a real `wrangler dev` handshake, instead of one that fails at runtime reaching for a Node built-in that isn't there.

stack.name: Astro 5
stack.reason: Islands architecture ships near-zero client JS on static content by default.

stack.name: React (islands only)
stack.reason: Used only where a component genuinely needs client-side state — chat, forms, toggles.

stack.name: TypeScript, hexagonal ports & adapters
stack.reason: core stays framework-free; swapping an adapter never touches a page.

stack.name: Tailwind CSS v4
stack.reason: Token-driven styling; every color and spacing value traces to one CSS custom-property file.

stack.name: Cloudflare Pages + Workers
stack.reason: Hybrid output: pages prerender to the CDN, /api/* runs as Workers functions.

stack.name: Cloudflare Workers AI + Vectorize
stack.reason: Free-tier chatbot provider and embedding index, with Anthropic as a config-swappable fallback.

stack.name: Cloudflare KV
stack.reason: Rate-limit storage for /api/contact and /api/chat, free tier, no separate database.

stack.name: Resend
stack.reason: Contact form email delivery via the REST API; the RESEND_API_KEY env binding keeps it out of the bundle.

stack.name: Cloudflare Turnstile
stack.reason: Server-side bot verification on /api/contact; sitekey is public, secret stays in the Worker env binding.

stack.name: Astro content collections (git-based CMS)
stack.reason: content/ is the CMS — MDX/JSON, versioned in git, edited three equivalent ways.

stack.name: Keystatic
stack.reason: Local content-editing UI mirroring the same zod schemas Astro's collections already validate.

stack.name: GSAP, Lenis, OGL (motion stack)
stack.reason: MIT-licensed; loaded lazily per page so content pages never pay for motion they don't use.

stack.name: Model Context Protocol (packages/mcp)
stack.reason: Publishes the same content and chatbot to AI agents as typed tools, not scraped HTML.

stack.name: Vitest + Playwright
stack.reason: Vitest for unit and architecture boundary tests; Playwright for cross-engine E2E across Chromium, WebKit, and mobile-WebKit.

changelog.title: Experience and career narrative published
changelog.detail: Scroll-driven career timeline and printable executive dossier go live, covering seven roles.

changelog.title: Projects, writing engine, and chatbot launch
changelog.detail: Project deep-dives, an RSS-backed writing engine with per-article publish kits, and a RAG chatbot built on Cloudflare Workers AI ship in one push.

changelog.title: Accessibility controls added
changelog.detail: A floating panel ships with reduce-motion, high-contrast, and larger-text toggles, backed by a published accessibility statement.

changelog.title: AI-readable surfaces ship
changelog.detail: Markdown twins for every page, an llms.txt file, and a JSON Resume endpoint go live. An AI agent can read this site cleanly without scraping HTML.

changelog.title: MCP server live at mcp.felipetavares.dev
changelog.detail: Career data and the chatbot are exposed as typed Model Context Protocol tools. Any MCP-capable agent can query roles, search articles, or start a grounded chat.

changelog.title: Brand identity and galaxy backdrop ship
changelog.detail: The owner's brand glyph replaces placeholder marks across favicon, OG image, and chat launcher. A WebGL galaxy layer sits behind all page content, tuned for light and dark themes.

changelog.title: /reading section opens
changelog.detail: A public log of books, articles, and papers, each with a verdict and rationale. Filterable by type and domain, with four verdict levels.

changelog.title: Site deployed to Cloudflare Pages at $0/month
changelog.detail: The full stack (Pages, Workers, KV, Vectorize, Workers AI) runs inside Cloudflare's free tier. Total recurring cost after the domain: $0/month.

changelog.title: Structured data and SEO pass
changelog.detail: Person, WebSite, ProfilePage, and Article JSON-LD added across all main pages. Desktop Lighthouse scores after the pass: 100/100/100/100.

changelog.title: Agent-discovery endpoints added
changelog.detail: A well-known API catalog, an MCP server card, and HTTP Link headers on the homepage let AI agents find the site's machine-readable surfaces without guessing paths.
